content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 2, 1990


Mr. Mark Neveau
2830 Barrington Terrace
Fremont, CA  94536

Re:  Your Request for Confirmation
     of Telephone Advice
     Our File No. I-90-595

Dear Mr. Neveau:

     On September 5, 1990, you telephoned for advice on behalf of
two city councilmembers concerning use of campaign funds under the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1  Your letter of September 11,
1990, correctly states the advice we gave you.

     The two councilmembers were successful in the last election.
They wish to use their remaining campaign funds on a variety of
expenditures listed in your letter, which mainly relate to
communications with constituents about community issues and
working.  Campaign funds may be used for expenditures associated
with holding office if they are reasonably related to a
legislative or governmental purpose.  (Section 85801.)  In some
instances, the expenditure must be directly related to a
legislative or governmental purpose.  Expenditures which confer a
substantial personal benefit must be directly related to a
legislative or governmental purpose.  (Section 85801.)
"Substantial personal benefit" means a direct personal benefit
with a value of more than $100 to an elected officer.  (Section
85800(2).)

     Most of the contemplated uses in your letter appear to be
reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose.  Two
require special consideration, however.


_______________
1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Item number three in your letter, video image training,
appears to be an expenditure that may confer a substantial
personal benefit to the officeholder. Thus, such an expenditure
must meet the "directly related" standard. Since the training is
specifically designed to promote effective communication during
televised city council meetings, it appears the training is
directly related to a legislative or governmental purpose.

In addition, you should be aware that if the campaign funds
are used to purchase a computer (item number one in your letter),
the title to the computer may not be in the officeholder's name or
in the name of a member of his or her family or the campaign
treasurer. The title should be in the name of the committee.
(Section 85805.)

In the near future, the Commission will be considering
regulations that will further define the standards for use of
campaign funds. Therefore, the advice given you may change based
on the Commission's actions and you may wish to verify the advice
in this letter at a later date.

I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further question regarding this
matter, please call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Peggy Bernardy
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:PB:dg

Your letter of September 11, 1990, correctly states the advice we gave you. The two councilmembers were successful in the last election. They wish to use their remaining campaign funds on a variety of expenditures listed in your letter, which mainly relate to communications with constituents about community issues and voting. Campaign funds may be used for expenditures associated with holding office if they are reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose. (Section 85801.) In some instances, the expenditure must be directly related to a legislative or governmental purpose. Expenditures which confer a substantial personal benefit must be directly related to a legislative or governmental purpose. (Section 85801.) "Substantial personal benefit" means a direct personal benefit with a value of more than $100 to an elected officer. (Section 85800(2).) Most of the contemplated uses in your letter appear to be reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose. Two require special consideration, however. 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. Item number three in your letter, video image training, appears to be an expenditure that may confer a substantial personal benefit to the officeholder. Thus, such an expenditure must meet the "directly related" standard. Since the training is specifically designed to promote effective communication during televised city council meetings, it appears the training is directly related to a legislative or governmental purpose. In addition, you should be aware that if the campaign funds are used to purchase a computer (item number one in your letter), the title to the computer may not be in the officeholder's name or in the name of a member of his or her family or the campaign treasurer. The title should be in the name of the committee. (Section 85805.) In the near future, the Commission will be considering regulations that will further define the standards for use of campaign funds. Therefore, the advice given you may change based on the Commission's actions and you may wish to verify the advice in this letter at a later date. I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you requested. If you have any further question regarding this matter, please call me at (916) 322-5901. Sincerely, Scott Hallabrin Acting General Counsel By: Peggy Bernardy Counsel, Legal Division
